Saudi Arabia & Vision 2030 — Data Analysis

DS 220 | Project #2 | Penn State | Data source: World Bank (1990–2023)

What is this about?

Saudi Arabia launched Vision 2030 in 2016 to reduce oil dependency and modernize the economy. This project analyzes 33 years of World Bank data to answer: is the transformation actually happening?

Dataset

World Bank Development Indicators for Saudi Arabia, 1990–2023. Covers GDP, oil rents, female employment, tourism, unemployment, and CO₂ emissions.

Questions

  1. How has GDP grown over 3 decades?
  2. Is oil dependency decreasing?
  3. How has female workforce participation changed?
  4. Is tourism growing?
  5. How has unemployment changed?
  6. Does GDP move with oil prices?
  7. How have CO₂ emissions changed?
  8. What does an overall Vision 2030 dashboard show?

Q1 — GDP Growth

GDP grew from $117B in 1990 to $1.1T in 2022. Still very tied to oil price cycles.

Q2 — Oil Dependency

Oil rents as % of GDP are slightly lower post-2016 but no major structural change yet.

Q3 — Female Workforce Participation

Biggest success: female participation nearly doubled from 17% to 34% since 2016.

Q4 — Tourism

Record 24.9M visitors in 2023. Strong recovery after COVID collapsed it to 1.7M in 2020.

Q5 — Unemployment

Stable at ~5.8% in 2023. Peaked at 7.4% during COVID. No dramatic change.

Q6 — Oil vs GDP

GDP still strongly tracks oil prices. The 2022 boom was oil-driven, not diversification.

Q7 — CO₂ Emissions

16.8 metric tons per person — 3.7x the world average. No meaningful improvement.

Q8 — Full Dashboard

Combined view: female employment and tourism are up. Oil still dominates GDP.

Key Takeaways

  • Win: Female workforce participation nearly doubled since 2016
  • Win: Tourism hit a record high in 2023
  • Mixed: Non-oil sectors are growing but slowly
  • Lagging: CO₂ emissions show no improvement
  • Overall: Social transformation is real. Economic diversification is still in progress.
